{{short description|Shinto shrine in Hiroshima Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox UNESCO World Heritage Site | WHS = Itsukushima Shinto Shrine | image = [[File:Itsukushima Shrine Torii Gate (13890465459).jpg|260px]] | Caption = The ''[[torii]]'' of Itsukushima Shrine, the site's most recognizable landmark, appears to float in the water. | Location = [[Itsukushima]], Japan | Criteria = Cultural: i, ii, iv, vi | ID = 776 | Year = 1996 | Area = 431.2 ha | Buffer_zone = 2,634.3 ha | Website = {{URL|http://www.en.itsukushimajinja.jp}} | Coordinates = {{coord|34|17|45|N|132|19|11|E}} | locmapin = Japan | embedded = {{Infobox Chinese | pic = Itsukushima Shrine (Chinese characters).svg | piccap = The name of Itsukushima Shrine in ''[[kanji]]'' | picupright = 0.65 | shinjitai = 厳島神社 | kyujitai = 嚴島神社 | hiragana = いつくしまじんじゃ | revhep = Itsukushima-jinja | child = yes }} }}
{{nihongo|'''Itsukushima Shrine'''|厳島神社|Itsukushima-jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] on the island of [[Itsukushima]] (popularly known as Miyajima), best known for its "floating" ''[[torii]]''.<ref name="nussbaum4072">[[Louis-Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]] (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA407 "''Itsukushima-jinja''"] in ''Japan Encyclopedia'', p. 407.</ref> It is in the city of {{ill|Hatsukaichi|en|Hatsukaichi|qid=Q736897|lt=Hatsukaichi|1=Hatsukaichi}}, in {{ill|Hiroshima Prefecture|en|Hiroshima Prefecture|qid=Q617375|lt=Hiroshima Prefecture|1=Hiroshima Prefecture}} in {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}, accessible from the mainland by ferry at [[Miyajimaguchi Station]]. The shrine complex is listed as a [[United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization|UNESCO]] [[World Heritage Site]], and the Japanese government has designated several buildings and possessions as [[National Treasures of Japan|National Treasures]].<ref name="cali-2013a"/>
The Itsukushima shrine is one of Japan's most popular tourist attractions. It is most famous for its dramatic gate, or ''torii'' on the outskirts of the shrine,<ref name="cali-2013a">{{Cite book|jstor=j.ctt6wqfhm|title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion|last1=Cali|first1=Joseph|last2=Dougill|first2=John|last3=Ciotti|first3=Geoff|date=2013|publisher=University of Hawai'i Press|isbn=9780824837136}}</ref> the sacred peaks of {{ill|Mount Misen|en|Mount Misen|qid=Q3109237|lt=Mount Misen|1=Mount Misen}}, extensive forests, and its ocean view.<ref name="nussbaum4072"/><ref name="ramsar-2017a">{{Cite web|url=https://www.ramsar.org/sites/default/files/documents/library/ramsar_whc_case_study_itsukushima_e.pdf|title=Ramsar and World Heritage Conventions: Converging towards success - Case study: Itsukushima Shinto Shrine, Japan|date=15 September 2017|website=Ramsar}}</ref> The shrine complex itself consists of two main buildings: the Honsha shrine and the Sessha Marodo-jinja, as well as 17 other different buildings and structures that help to distinguish it.<ref name="ramsar-2017a" />
==History==
=== Origin === ''Itsukushima jinja'' was the chief Shinto shrine (''[[ichinomiya]]'') of {{ill|Aki Province|en|Aki Province|qid=Q965029|lt=Aki Province|1=Aki Province}}.<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''", p. 3] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130517061440/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf|date=2013-05-17}}; retrieved 2012-11-20.</ref>
This shrine is one of the "Three Great Shrines of Aki Province", along with [[Take Shrine]] and [[Hayatani Shrine]].<ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=LPq-lhGqzgEC&q=Hayatani+Shrine |title=Hiroshima |date=1986 |publisher=Hiroshima City |language=en}}</ref>
The first ''torii'' on the site is said to have been erected in 593, supposedly by Saeki Kuramoto during the reign of {{ill|Empress Suiko|en|Empress Suiko|qid=Q298057|lt=Empress Suiko|1=Empress Suiko}} (592–628 {{ill|Common Era|en|Common Era|qid=Q208141|lt=CE|1=Common Era}}).<ref name="cali-2013a"/> Although a ''torii'' has been in place at the site since 1168, the current structure dates to 1875.<ref name="cali-2013a" /> The present shrine has been popularly attributed to [[Taira no Kiyomori]], a prominent noble of the Imperial Court and later Chancellor ({{ill|Daijō-daijin|en|Daijō-daijin|qid=Q1157586|lt=Daijō-daijin|1=Daijō-daijin}}), who contributed heavily to the construction of the shrine during his time as governor of Aki Province in 1168.<ref name="sadler-2009a">{{Cite book|title=A Short History of Japanese Architecture|year=2009|url=https://archive.org/details/japanesearchitec0000sadl|url-access=registration|last=Sadler|first=A. L.}}</ref> Another renowned patron of the shrine was [[Mōri Motonari]], lord of {{ill|Chōshū Domain|en|Chōshū Domain|qid=Q876267|lt=Chōshū Domain|1=Chōshū Domain}},<ref name="cali-2013a"/><ref name="sadler-2009a"/> who was responsible for rebuilding the {{lang|ja-latn|[[honden]]}} in 1571. As a result of waging war against [[Sue Takafusa]] there in 1555, Motonari is said to have tainted the island's grounds by battling on the island.<ref name="cali-2013a"/> Spilling blood violated the strict taboos meant to preserve the sacred purity associated with Shinto shrines.<ref name="sadler-2009a"/> The only surviving structure in Itsukushima shrine from the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|qid=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}} is the Kyakuden or "Guest-God's Shrine".
=== Kiyomori === It was not uncommon during the 12th century for the nobility to build shrines or take on other architectural projects in order to "reflect their power and splendor."<ref name="calza-2002a">{{Cite book|title=Japan Style|last=Calza|first=Gian Carlo|publisher=Phaidon|year=2002|isbn=978-1100744452|pages=190}}</ref> The [[Taira clan]] are known specifically for their involvement in maritime trade with the [[Song dynasty]] (960–1279) and for attempting to monopolize overseas trade along the Inland Sea.<ref name="shively-1999a">{{Cite book|title=The Cambridge History of Japan, Volume 2: Heian Japan|last=Shively|first=Donald H.|publisher=Cambridge University Press|year=1999|pages=635}}</ref>
Kiyomori was at the height of his power when he established the Taira dominion over the island. He "ordered construction of the main hall of Itsukushima shrine as a display of reverence for the tutelary god of navigation and to serve as a base for maritime activities..."<ref name="shively-1999a" /> Miyajima soon became the Taira family shrine.<ref name="cali-2013a"/> Supposedly, Kiyomori chose the location also for the reason to further establish himself in the Heian aristocracy as one who deviated from the social norms of [[Shinto]] pilgrimage .<ref name="blair-2013a">{{Cite journal|last=BLAIR|first=HEATHER|date=2013|title=Rites and Rule: Kiyomori at Itsukushima and Fukuhara|journal=Harvard Journal of Asiatic Studies|volume=73|issue=1|pages=1–42|issn=0073-0548|jstor=44478243}}</ref> He lavished great wealth upon Itsukushima, and he enjoyed showing the place to his friends and colleagues, or even to royal personages..."<ref name="Sansom">{{Cite book|title=A History of Japan to 1334|url=https://archive.org/details/historyofjapanto00sans|url-access=registration|last=Sansom|first=George|publisher=Stanford University Press|year=1958|isbn=978-0804705233|page=[https://archive.org/details/historyofjapanto00sans/page/276 276]}}</ref>
It is also said that [[:en:Kiyomori|Kiyomori]] rebuilt the shrine on account of a dream he had of an old monk who promised him dominion over Japan if he constructed a shrine on the island of Miyajima, and pay homage to its [[kami]] who are enshrined there for his success in life.<ref name="sadler-2009a"/><ref name="cali-2013a"/> The renovations funded by the Taira allowed for Itsukushima to "grow into an important religious complex."<ref name="blair-2013a"/>[[File:Itsukushima Shrine at high tide.jpg|thumb|The Itsukushima Shrine at high tide, when it appears to float on the water]]
==Religious significance== The Itsukushima shrine is dedicated to the three daughters of [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susano-o no Mikoto]]: Ichikishima-hime no mikoto, Tagorihime no mikoto, and Tagitsuhime no mikoto. Otherwise known as the ''sanjoshin'' or "three female deities", these Shinto deities are the goddesses of seas and storms. Kiyomori believed the goddesses to be "manifestations of [[Kannon]]," therefore the island was understood as the home of the {{ill|Bodhisattva|en|Bodhisattva|qid=Q178149|lt=Bodhisattva|1=Bodhisattva}}.<ref name="blair-2013a"/> In Japanese, the word Itsukushima translates to "island dedicated to the gods."<ref name="cali-2013a"/> The island itself is also considered to be a god, which is why the shrine was built on the outskirts of the island.<ref name="cali-2013a"/> Adding to its sanctity, Mount Misen is the tallest peak at about 1,755 feet.<ref name="cali-2013a"/> Tourists can either hike or take a ropeway to the top.<ref name="cali-2013a"/>
Its treasures include the celebrated [[List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)|Heike Nōkyō]], or "Sutras dedicated by the [[Taira clan|House of Taira]]." These consist of thirty-two scrolls, on which the Lotus, Amida, and Heart sutras have been copied by Kiyomori, his sons, and other members of the family, each completing the transcription of one scroll, and it was "decorated with silver, gold, and mother-of-pearl by himself [Kiyomori] and other members of his clan."<ref name="cali-2013a"/>
Originally Itsukushima was a pure Shinto shrine "where no births or deaths were allowed to cause pollution". Because the island itself has been considered sacred, commoners were not allowed to set foot on it throughout much of its history to maintain its purity. Retaining the purity of the shrine is so important that since 1878, no deaths or births have been permitted near it.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.gojapango.com/travel/miyajima_itsukushima.htm|title=Itsukushima|year=2010|work=GoJapanGo|access-date=17 March 2011|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120609204136/http://www.gojapango.com/travel/miyajima_itsukushima.htm|archive-date=9 June 2012|url-status=dead}}</ref> To this day, pregnant women are supposed to retreat to the mainland as the day of delivery approaches, as are the terminally ill or the very elderly whose passing has become imminent. [[:en:Burials|Burials]] on the island are forbidden. To allow pilgrims to approach, the shrine was built like a [[pier]] over the water, so that it appeared to float, separate from the land.<ref>{{Cite book|title=The Ritual Process: Structure and Anti-structure|url=https://archive.org/details/ritualprocess0000unse|url-access=registration|last=Turner|first=Victor W.|publisher=Aldine Pub.|year=1969|isbn=9780202010434|place=Chicago}}</ref> The red entrance gate, or ''[[torii]]'', was built over the water for much the same reason. Commoners had to steer their boats through the ''torii'' before approaching the shrine.[[Image:Itsukushima Shinto Shrine.jpg|center|700px|thumb|View from the ''[[torii]]'']]
== Architecture == [[File:Tori_gate_01.jpg|thumb|left|The ''torii'' gate, accessible from the island during low tide]]
Japan has gone to great lengths to preserve the twelfth-century-style architecture of the Shrine throughout history. The shrine was designed and built according to the ''[[Shinden-zukuri]]'' style, equipped with pier-like structures over the [[Matsushima]] bay in order to create the illusion of floating on the water, separate from island, which could be approached by the devout "like a palace on the sea."<ref name="sadler-2009a"/> This idea of intertwining architecture and nature is reflective of a popular trend during the 16th century as well as the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}} in which Japanese structures tended to "follow after their environment," often allowing trees, water, and other forms of natural beauty to enter into the decor of homes and buildings. That led to a far more intimate relationship between the two.<ref name="calza-2002a"/>
[[File:Itsukushima-jinja_honden.jpeg|thumb|Itsukushima {{lang|ja-latn|[[honden]]}}]] [[File:Itsukushima_Honden_Haiden.jpg|thumb|Itsukushima ''[[Haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'']]
The most recognizable and celebrated feature of the Itsukushima shrine, is its {{convert|50|ft|m|0|adj=on}}-tall vermilion otorii gate ("great gate"), built of decay-resistant [[:en:Cinnamomum camphora|camphor wood]].<ref name="cali-2013a"/> The placement of an additional leg in front of and behind each main pillar identifies the ''torii'' as reflecting the style of ''[[Ryōbu Shintō]]'' (dual Shinto), a medieval school of esoteric {{ill|Buddhism in Japan|en|Buddhism in Japan|qid=Q736311|lt=Buddhism in Japan|1=Buddhism in Japan}} associated with the {{ill|Shingon Buddhism|en|Shingon Buddhism|qid=Q550182|lt=Shingon Sect|1=Shingon Buddhism}}. The ''torii'' appears to be floating only at high [[tide]]. When the tide is low, it is approachable by foot from the island. Gathering [[shellfish]] near the gate is popular at low tide. At night, powerful lights on the shore illuminate the structure. The current ''torii'' dates to 1875, although there has been one on that site since 1168.<ref name="cali-2013a" />
[[Shinto architecture]] has many distinct parts, most of which include the shrine's {{lang|ja-latn|[[honden]]}} (main hall) and the unusually long ''[[Haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' (main oratory), and its equally long ''[[Heiden (Shinto)|heiden]]'' (offertory hall). The {{lang|ja-latn|honden}} "is an eight-by-four bay structure with a kirizuma roof surfaced in {{ill|Cypress|en|Cypress|qid=Q14169641|lt=Cypress|1=Cypress}} bark."<ref name="cali-2013a" /> Its walls are decorated in white [[stucco]], and were constructed using a process requiring fifteen coats of white stucco, with vermilion woodwork.<ref name="cali-2013a" />
Extending from the sides of the ''haraiden'' of the main shrine is a ''[[noh]]'' stage which dates from 1590.<ref name="nussbaum4072"/> ''Noh'' theater performances have long been used to pay homage to the gods through the ritual acting out of key events in Shinto myth.
On September 5, 2004, the shrine was severely damaged by [[2004 Pacific typhoon season#Typhoon Songda (Nina)|Typhoon Songda]]. The boardwalks and roof were partially destroyed, and the shrine was temporarily closed for repairs. Today anyone can visit the shrine at a cost of 300 yen.<ref name="cali-2013a" />

{{short description|Shinto shrine in Hiroshima Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox UNESCO World Heritage Site | WHS = Itsukushima Shinto Shrine | image = [[File:Itsukushima Shrine Torii Gate (13890465459).jpg|260px]] | Caption = The ''[[torii]]'' of Itsukushima Shrine, the site's most recognizable landmark, appears to float in the water. | Location = [[Itsukushima]], Japan | Criteria = Cultural: i, ii, iv, vi | ID = 776 | Year = 1996 | Area = 431.2 ha | Buffer_zone = 2,634.3 ha | Website = {{URL|http://www.en.itsukushimajinja.jp}} | Coordinates = {{coord|34|17|45|N|132|19|11|E}} | locmapin = Japan | embedded = {{Infobox Chinese | pic = Itsukushima Shrine (Chinese characters).svg | piccap = The name of Itsukushima Shrine in ''[[kanji]]'' | picupright = 0.65 | shinjitai = 厳島神社 | kyujitai = 嚴島神社 | hiragana = いつくしまじんじゃ | revhep = Itsukushima-jinja | child = yes }} }}
{{nihongo|'''Itsukushima Shrine'''|厳島神社|Itsukushima-jinja}} is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} on the island of [[Itsukushima]] (popularly known as Miyajima), best known for its "floating" ''[[torii]]''.<ref name="nussbaum4072">{{ill|Louis Frédéric|en|Louis Frédéric|qid=Q726040|lt=Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric|1=Louis Frédéric}} (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA407 "''Itsukushima-jinja''"] in ''Japan Encyclopedia'', p. 407.</ref> It is in the city of {{ill|Hatsukaichi|en|Hatsukaichi|qid=Q736897|lt=Hatsukaichi|1=Hatsukaichi}}, in {{ill|Hiroshima Prefecture|en|Hiroshima Prefecture|qid=Q617375|lt=Hiroshima Prefecture|1=Hiroshima Prefecture}} in {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}, accessible from the mainland by ferry at {{ill|Miyajimaguchi Station|en|Miyajimaguchi Station|qid=Q875417|lt=Miyajimaguchi Station|1=Miyajimaguchi Station}}. The shrine complex is listed as a [[:en:United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization|UNESCO]] {{ill|World Heritage Site|en|World Heritage Site|qid=Q9259|lt=World Heritage Site|1=World Heritage Site}}, and the Japanese government has designated several buildings and possessions as {{ill|National Treasure (Japan)|en|National Treasure (Japan)|qid=Q1139795|lt=National Treasures|1=National Treasure (Japan)}}.<ref name="cali-2013a"/>
The Itsukushima shrine is one of Japan's most popular tourist attractions. It is most famous for its dramatic gate, or ''torii'' on the outskirts of the shrine,<ref name="cali-2013a">{{Cite book|jstor=j.ctt6wqfhm|title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion|last1=Cali|first1=Joseph|last2=Dougill|first2=John|last3=Ciotti|first3=Geoff|date=2013|publisher=University of Hawai'i Press|isbn=9780824837136}}</ref> the sacred peaks of {{ill|Mount Misen|en|Mount Misen|qid=Q3109237|lt=Mount Misen|1=Mount Misen}}, extensive forests, and its ocean view.<ref name="nussbaum4072"/><ref name="ramsar-2017a">{{Cite web|url=https://www.ramsar.org/sites/default/files/documents/library/ramsar_whc_case_study_itsukushima_e.pdf|title=Ramsar and World Heritage Conventions: Converging towards success - Case study: Itsukushima Shinto Shrine, Japan|date=15 September 2017|website=Ramsar}}</ref> The shrine complex itself consists of two main buildings: the Honsha shrine and the Sessha Marodo-jinja, as well as 17 other different buildings and structures that help to distinguish it.<ref name="ramsar-2017a" />
==History==
=== Origin === ''Itsukushima jinja'' was the chief Shinto shrine (''[[ichinomiya]]'') of {{ill|Aki Province|en|Aki Province|qid=Q965029|lt=Aki Province|1=Aki Province}}.<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''", p. 3] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130517061440/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf|date=2013-05-17}}; retrieved 2012-11-20.</ref>
This shrine is one of the "Three Great Shrines of Aki Province", along with [[Take Shrine]] and [[Hayatani Shrine]].<ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=LPq-lhGqzgEC&q=Hayatani+Shrine |title=Hiroshima |date=1986 |publisher=Hiroshima City |language=en}}</ref>
The first ''torii'' on the site is said to have been erected in 593, supposedly by Saeki Kuramoto during the reign of {{ill|Empress Suiko|en|Empress Suiko|qid=Q298057|lt=Empress Suiko|1=Empress Suiko}} (592–628 {{ill|Common Era|en|Common Era|qid=Q208141|lt=CE|1=Common Era}}).<ref name="cali-2013a"/> Although a ''torii'' has been in place at the site since 1168, the current structure dates to 1875.<ref name="cali-2013a" /> The present shrine has been popularly attributed to [[Taira no Kiyomori]], a prominent noble of the Imperial Court and later Chancellor ({{ill|Daijō-daijin|en|Daijō-daijin|qid=Q1157586|lt=Daijō-daijin|1=Daijō-daijin}}), who contributed heavily to the construction of the shrine during his time as governor of Aki Province in 1168.<ref name="sadler-2009a">{{Cite book|title=A Short History of Japanese Architecture|year=2009|url=https://archive.org/details/japanesearchitec0000sadl|url-access=registration|last=Sadler|first=A. L.}}</ref> Another renowned patron of the shrine was {{ill|Mōri Motonari|en|Mōri Motonari|qid=Q1156545|lt=Mōri Motonari|1=Mōri Motonari}}, lord of {{ill|Chōshū Domain|en|Chōshū Domain|qid=Q876267|lt=Chōshū Domain|1=Chōshū Domain}},<ref name="cali-2013a"/><ref name="sadler-2009a"/> who was responsible for rebuilding the {{lang|ja-latn|[[honden]]}} in 1571. As a result of waging war against [[:en:Sue Takafusa|Sue Takafusa]] there in 1555, Motonari is said to have tainted the island's grounds by battling on the island.<ref name="cali-2013a"/> Spilling blood violated the strict taboos meant to preserve the sacred purity associated with Shinto shrines.<ref name="sadler-2009a"/> The only surviving structure in Itsukushima shrine from the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|qid=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}} is the Kyakuden or "Guest-God's Shrine".
=== Kiyomori === It was not uncommon during the 12th century for the nobility to build shrines or take on other architectural projects in order to "reflect their power and splendor."<ref name="calza-2002a">{{Cite book|title=Japan Style|last=Calza|first=Gian Carlo|publisher=Phaidon|year=2002|isbn=978-1100744452|pages=190}}</ref> The [[Taira clan]] are known specifically for their involvement in maritime trade with the {{ill|Song dynasty|en|Song dynasty|qid=Q7462|lt=Song dynasty|1=Song dynasty}} (960–1279) and for attempting to monopolize overseas trade along the Inland Sea.<ref name="shively-1999a">{{Cite book|title=The Cambridge History of Japan, Volume 2: Heian Japan|last=Shively|first=Donald H.|publisher=Cambridge University Press|year=1999|pages=635}}</ref>
Kiyomori was at the height of his power when he established the Taira dominion over the island. He "ordered construction of the main hall of Itsukushima shrine as a display of reverence for the tutelary god of navigation and to serve as a base for maritime activities..."<ref name="shively-1999a" /> Miyajima soon became the Taira family shrine.<ref name="cali-2013a"/> Supposedly, Kiyomori chose the location also for the reason to further establish himself in the Heian aristocracy as one who deviated from the social norms of [[Shinto]] pilgrimage .<ref name="blair-2013a">{{Cite journal|last=BLAIR|first=HEATHER|date=2013|title=Rites and Rule: Kiyomori at Itsukushima and Fukuhara|journal=Harvard Journal of Asiatic Studies|volume=73|issue=1|pages=1–42|issn=0073-0548|jstor=44478243}}</ref> He lavished great wealth upon Itsukushima, and he enjoyed showing the place to his friends and colleagues, or even to royal personages..."<ref name="Sansom">{{Cite book|title=A History of Japan to 1334|url=https://archive.org/details/historyofjapanto00sans|url-access=registration|last=Sansom|first=George|publisher=Stanford University Press|year=1958|isbn=978-0804705233|page=[https://archive.org/details/historyofjapanto00sans/page/276 276]}}</ref>
It is also said that [[:en:Kiyomori|Kiyomori]] rebuilt the shrine on account of a dream he had of an old monk who promised him dominion over Japan if he constructed a shrine on the island of Miyajima, and pay homage to its [[kami]] who are enshrined there for his success in life.<ref name="sadler-2009a"/><ref name="cali-2013a"/> The renovations funded by the Taira allowed for Itsukushima to "grow into an important religious complex."<ref name="blair-2013a"/>[[File:Itsukushima Shrine at high tide.jpg|thumb|The Itsukushima Shrine at high tide, when it appears to float on the water]]
==Religious significance== The Itsukushima shrine is dedicated to the three daughters of [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susano-o no Mikoto]]: Ichikishima-hime no mikoto, Tagorihime no mikoto, and Tagitsuhime no mikoto. Otherwise known as the ''sanjoshin'' or "three female deities", these Shinto deities are the goddesses of seas and storms. Kiyomori believed the goddesses to be "manifestations of [[Kannon]]," therefore the island was understood as the home of the {{ill|Bodhisattva|en|Bodhisattva|qid=Q178149|lt=Bodhisattva|1=Bodhisattva}}.<ref name="blair-2013a"/> In Japanese, the word Itsukushima translates to "island dedicated to the gods."<ref name="cali-2013a"/> The island itself is also considered to be a god, which is why the shrine was built on the outskirts of the island.<ref name="cali-2013a"/> Adding to its sanctity, Mount Misen is the tallest peak at about 1,755 feet.<ref name="cali-2013a"/> Tourists can either hike or take a ropeway to the top.<ref name="cali-2013a"/>
Its treasures include the celebrated {{ill|en:List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)|en|en:List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)|qid=Q3245518|lt=Heike Nōkyō|1=List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)}}, or "Sutras dedicated by the [[Taira clan|House of Taira]]." These consist of thirty-two scrolls, on which the Lotus, Amida, and Heart sutras have been copied by Kiyomori, his sons, and other members of the family, each completing the transcription of one scroll, and it was "decorated with silver, gold, and mother-of-pearl by himself [Kiyomori] and other members of his clan."<ref name="cali-2013a"/>
Originally Itsukushima was a pure Shinto shrine "where no births or deaths were allowed to cause pollution". Because the island itself has been considered sacred, commoners were not allowed to set foot on it throughout much of its history to maintain its purity. Retaining the purity of the shrine is so important that since 1878, no deaths or births have been permitted near it.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.gojapango.com/travel/miyajima_itsukushima.htm|title=Itsukushima|year=2010|work=GoJapanGo|access-date=17 March 2011|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120609204136/http://www.gojapango.com/travel/miyajima_itsukushima.htm|archive-date=9 June 2012|url-status=dead}}</ref> To this day, pregnant women are supposed to retreat to the mainland as the day of delivery approaches, as are the terminally ill or the very elderly whose passing has become imminent. [[:en:Burials|Burials]] on the island are forbidden. To allow pilgrims to approach, the shrine was built like a {{ill|Pier|en|Pier|qid=Q863454|lt=Pier|1=Pier}} over the water, so that it appeared to float, separate from the land.<ref>{{Cite book|title=The Ritual Process: Structure and Anti-structure|url=https://archive.org/details/ritualprocess0000unse|url-access=registration|last=Turner|first=Victor W.|publisher=Aldine Pub.|year=1969|isbn=9780202010434|place=Chicago}}</ref> The red entrance gate, or ''[[torii]]'', was built over the water for much the same reason. Commoners had to steer their boats through the ''torii'' before approaching the shrine.[[Image:Itsukushima Shinto Shrine.jpg|center|700px|thumb|View from the ''[[torii]]'']]
== Architecture == [[File:Tori_gate_01.jpg|thumb|left|The ''torii'' gate, accessible from the island during low tide]]
Japan has gone to great lengths to preserve the twelfth-century-style architecture of the Shrine throughout history. The shrine was designed and built according to the ''{{ill|Shinden-zukuri|en|Shinden-zukuri|qid=Q2975225|lt=Shinden-zukuri|1=Shinden-zukuri}}'' style, equipped with pier-like structures over the {{ill|Matsushima|en|Matsushima|qid=Q1144855|lt=Matsushima|1=Matsushima}} bay in order to create the illusion of floating on the water, separate from island, which could be approached by the devout "like a palace on the sea."<ref name="sadler-2009a"/> This idea of intertwining architecture and nature is reflective of a popular trend during the 16th century as well as the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}} in which Japanese structures tended to "follow after their environment," often allowing trees, water, and other forms of natural beauty to enter into the decor of homes and buildings. That led to a far more intimate relationship between the two.<ref name="calza-2002a"/>
[[File:Itsukushima-jinja_honden.jpeg|thumb|Itsukushima {{lang|ja-latn|[[honden]]}}]] [[File:Itsukushima_Honden_Haiden.jpg|thumb|Itsukushima ''[[Haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'']]
The most recognizable and celebrated feature of the Itsukushima shrine, is its {{convert|50|ft|m|0|adj=on}}-tall vermilion otorii gate ("great gate"), built of decay-resistant [[:en:Cinnamomum camphora|camphor wood]].<ref name="cali-2013a"/> The placement of an additional leg in front of and behind each main pillar identifies the ''torii'' as reflecting the style of ''[[:en:Ryōbu Shintō|Ryōbu Shintō]]'' (dual Shinto), a medieval school of esoteric {{ill|Buddhism in Japan|en|Buddhism in Japan|qid=Q736311|lt=Buddhism in Japan|1=Buddhism in Japan}} associated with the {{ill|Shingon Buddhism|en|Shingon Buddhism|qid=Q550182|lt=Shingon Sect|1=Shingon Buddhism}}. The ''torii'' appears to be floating only at high {{ill|Tide|en|Tide|qid=Q23384|lt=Tide|1=Tide}}. When the tide is low, it is approachable by foot from the island. Gathering {{ill|Shellfish|en|Shellfish|qid=Q6501235|lt=Shellfish|1=Shellfish}} near the gate is popular at low tide. At night, powerful lights on the shore illuminate the structure. The current ''torii'' dates to 1875, although there has been one on that site since 1168.<ref name="cali-2013a" />
{{ill|Shinto architecture|en|Shinto architecture|qid=Q3080012|lt=Shinto architecture|1=Shinto architecture}} has many distinct parts, most of which include the shrine's {{lang|ja-latn|[[honden]]}} (main hall) and the unusually long ''[[Haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' (main oratory), and its equally long ''[[Heiden (Shinto)|heiden]]'' (offertory hall). The {{lang|ja-latn|honden}} "is an eight-by-four bay structure with a kirizuma roof surfaced in {{ill|Cypress|en|Cypress|qid=Q14169641|lt=Cypress|1=Cypress}} bark."<ref name="cali-2013a" /> Its walls are decorated in white {{ill|Stucco|en|Stucco|qid=Q33526|lt=Stucco|1=Stucco}}, and were constructed using a process requiring fifteen coats of white stucco, with vermilion woodwork.<ref name="cali-2013a" />
Extending from the sides of the ''haraiden'' of the main shrine is a ''{{ill|Noh|en|Noh|qid=Q226887|lt=Noh|1=Noh}}'' stage which dates from 1590.<ref name="nussbaum4072"/> ''Noh'' theater performances have long been used to pay homage to the gods through the ritual acting out of key events in Shinto myth.
On September 5, 2004, the shrine was severely damaged by [[2004 Pacific typhoon season#Typhoon Songda (Nina)|Typhoon Songda]]. The boardwalks and roof were partially destroyed, and the shrine was temporarily closed for repairs. Today anyone can visit the shrine at a cost of 300 yen.<ref name="cali-2013a" />

{{short description|Shinto shrine in Hiroshima Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox UNESCO World Heritage Site | WHS = Itsukushima Shinto Shrine | image = [[File:Itsukushima Shrine Torii Gate (13890465459).jpg|260px]] | Caption = The ''[[torii]]'' of Itsukushima Shrine, the site's most recognizable landmark, appears to float in the water. | Location = [[Itsukushima]], Japan | Criteria = Cultural: i, ii, iv, vi | ID = 776 | Year = 1996 | Area = 431.2 ha | Buffer_zone = 2,634.3 ha | Website = {{URL|http://www.en.itsukushimajinja.jp}} | Coordinates = {{coord|34|17|45|N|132|19|11|E}} | locmapin = Japan | embedded = {{Infobox Chinese | pic = Itsukushima Shrine (Chinese characters).svg | piccap = The name of Itsukushima Shrine in ''[[kanji]]'' | picupright = 0.65 | shinjitai = 厳島神社 | kyujitai = 嚴島神社 | hiragana = いつくしまじんじゃ | revhep = Itsukushima-jinja | child = yes }} }}
{{nihongo|'''Itsukushima Shrine'''|厳島神社|Itsukushima-jinja}} is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} on the island of [[Itsukushima]] (popularly known as Miyajima), best known for its "floating" ''[[torii]]''.<ref name="nussbaum4072">{{ill|Louis Frédéric|en|Louis Frédéric|qid=Q726040|lt=Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric|1=Louis Frédéric}} (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA407 "''Itsukushima-jinja''"] in ''Japan Encyclopedia'', p. 407.</ref> It is in the city of {{ill|Hatsukaichi|en|Hatsukaichi|qid=Q736897|lt=Hatsukaichi|1=Hatsukaichi}}, in {{ill|Hiroshima Prefecture|en|Hiroshima Prefecture|qid=Q617375|lt=Hiroshima Prefecture|1=Hiroshima Prefecture}} in {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}, accessible from the mainland by ferry at {{ill|Miyajimaguchi Station|en|Miyajimaguchi Station|qid=Q875417|lt=Miyajimaguchi Station|1=Miyajimaguchi Station}}. The shrine complex is listed as a [[:en:United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization|UNESCO]] {{ill|World Heritage Site|en|World Heritage Site|qid=Q9259|lt=World Heritage Site|1=World Heritage Site}}, and the Japanese government has designated several buildings and possessions as {{ill|National Treasure (Japan)|en|National Treasure (Japan)|qid=Q1139795|lt=National Treasures|1=National Treasure (Japan)}}.<ref name="cali-2013a"/>
The Itsukushima shrine is one of Japan's most popular tourist attractions. It is most famous for its dramatic gate, or ''torii'' on the outskirts of the shrine,<ref name="cali-2013a">{{Cite book|jstor=j.ctt6wqfhm|title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion|last1=Cali|first1=Joseph|last2=Dougill|first2=John|last3=Ciotti|first3=Geoff|date=2013|publisher=University of Hawai'i Press|isbn=9780824837136}}</ref> the sacred peaks of {{ill|Mount Misen|en|Mount Misen|qid=Q3109237|lt=Mount Misen|1=Mount Misen}}, extensive forests, and its ocean view.<ref name="nussbaum4072"/><ref name="ramsar-2017a">{{Cite web|url=https://www.ramsar.org/sites/default/files/documents/library/ramsar_whc_case_study_itsukushima_e.pdf|title=Ramsar and World Heritage Conventions: Converging towards success - Case study: Itsukushima Shinto Shrine, Japan|date=15 September 2017|website=Ramsar}}</ref> The shrine complex itself consists of two main buildings: the Honsha shrine and the Sessha Marodo-jinja, as well as 17 other different buildings and structures that help to distinguish it.<ref name="ramsar-2017a" />
==History==
=== Origin === ''Itsukushima jinja'' was the chief Shinto shrine (''[[ichinomiya]]'') of {{ill|Aki Province|en|Aki Province|qid=Q965029|lt=Aki Province|1=Aki Province}}.<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''", p. 3] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130517061440/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf|date=2013-05-17}}; retrieved 2012-11-20.</ref>
This shrine is one of the "Three Great Shrines of Aki Province", along with [[Take Shrine]] and [[Hayatani Shrine]].<ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=LPq-lhGqzgEC&q=Hayatani+Shrine |title=Hiroshima |date=1986 |publisher=Hiroshima City |language=en}}</ref>
The first ''torii'' on the site is said to have been erected in 593, supposedly by Saeki Kuramoto during the reign of {{ill|Empress Suiko|en|Empress Suiko|qid=Q298057|lt=Empress Suiko|1=Empress Suiko}} (592–628 {{ill|Common Era|en|Common Era|qid=Q208141|lt=CE|1=Common Era}}).<ref name="cali-2013a"/> Although a ''torii'' has been in place at the site since 1168, the current structure dates to 1875.<ref name="cali-2013a" /> The present shrine has been popularly attributed to [[Taira no Kiyomori]], a prominent noble of the Imperial Court and later Chancellor ({{ill|Daijō-daijin|en|Daijō-daijin|qid=Q1157586|lt=Daijō-daijin|1=Daijō-daijin}}), who contributed heavily to the construction of the shrine during his time as governor of Aki Province in 1168.<ref name="sadler-2009a">{{Cite book|title=A Short History of Japanese Architecture|year=2009|url=https://archive.org/details/japanesearchitec0000sadl|url-access=registration|last=Sadler|first=A. L.}}</ref> Another renowned patron of the shrine was {{ill|Mōri Motonari|en|Mōri Motonari|qid=Q1156545|lt=Mōri Motonari|1=Mōri Motonari}}, lord of {{ill|Chōshū Domain|en|Chōshū Domain|qid=Q876267|lt=Chōshū Domain|1=Chōshū Domain}},<ref name="cali-2013a"/><ref name="sadler-2009a"/> who was responsible for rebuilding the {{lang|ja-latn|[[honden]]}} in 1571. As a result of waging war against [[:en:Sue Takafusa|Sue Takafusa]] there in 1555, Motonari is said to have tainted the island's grounds by battling on the island.<ref name="cali-2013a"/> Spilling blood violated the strict taboos meant to preserve the sacred purity associated with Shinto shrines.<ref name="sadler-2009a"/> The only surviving structure in Itsukushima shrine from the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|qid=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}} is the Kyakuden or "Guest-God's Shrine".
=== Kiyomori === It was not uncommon during the 12th century for the nobility to build shrines or take on other architectural projects in order to "reflect their power and splendor."<ref name="calza-2002a">{{Cite book|title=Japan Style|last=Calza|first=Gian Carlo|publisher=Phaidon|year=2002|isbn=978-1100744452|pages=190}}</ref> The [[Taira clan]] are known specifically for their involvement in maritime trade with the {{ill|Song dynasty|en|Song dynasty|qid=Q7462|lt=Song dynasty|1=Song dynasty}} (960–1279) and for attempting to monopolize overseas trade along the Inland Sea.<ref name="shively-1999a">{{Cite book|title=The Cambridge History of Japan, Volume 2: Heian Japan|last=Shively|first=Donald H.|publisher=Cambridge University Press|year=1999|pages=635}}</ref>
Kiyomori was at the height of his power when he established the Taira dominion over the island. He "ordered construction of the main hall of Itsukushima shrine as a display of reverence for the tutelary god of navigation and to serve as a base for maritime activities..."<ref name="shively-1999a" /> Miyajima soon became the Taira family shrine.<ref name="cali-2013a"/> Supposedly, Kiyomori chose the location also for the reason to further establish himself in the Heian aristocracy as one who deviated from the social norms of [[Shinto]] pilgrimage .<ref name="blair-2013a">{{Cite journal|last=BLAIR|first=HEATHER|date=2013|title=Rites and Rule: Kiyomori at Itsukushima and Fukuhara|journal=Harvard Journal of Asiatic Studies|volume=73|issue=1|pages=1–42|issn=0073-0548|jstor=44478243}}</ref> He lavished great wealth upon Itsukushima, and he enjoyed showing the place to his friends and colleagues, or even to royal personages..."<ref name="Sansom">{{Cite book|title=A History of Japan to 1334|url=https://archive.org/details/historyofjapanto00sans|url-access=registration|last=Sansom|first=George|publisher=Stanford University Press|year=1958|isbn=978-0804705233|page=[https://archive.org/details/historyofjapanto00sans/page/276 276]}}</ref>
It is also said that [[:en:Kiyomori|Kiyomori]] rebuilt the shrine on account of a dream he had of an old monk who promised him dominion over Japan if he constructed a shrine on the island of Miyajima, and pay homage to its [[kami]] who are enshrined there for his success in life.<ref name="sadler-2009a"/><ref name="cali-2013a"/> The renovations funded by the Taira allowed for Itsukushima to "grow into an important religious complex."<ref name="blair-2013a"/>[[File:Itsukushima Shrine at high tide.jpg|thumb|The Itsukushima Shrine at high tide, when it appears to float on the water]]
==Religious significance== The Itsukushima shrine is dedicated to the three daughters of [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susano-o no Mikoto]]: Ichikishima-hime no mikoto, Tagorihime no mikoto, and Tagitsuhime no mikoto. Otherwise known as the ''sanjoshin'' or "three female deities", these Shinto deities are the goddesses of seas and storms. Kiyomori believed the goddesses to be "manifestations of [[Kannon]]," therefore the island was understood as the home of the {{ill|Bodhisattva|en|Bodhisattva|qid=Q178149|lt=Bodhisattva|1=Bodhisattva}}.<ref name="blair-2013a"/> In Japanese, the word Itsukushima translates to "island dedicated to the gods."<ref name="cali-2013a"/> The island itself is also considered to be a god, which is why the shrine was built on the outskirts of the island.<ref name="cali-2013a"/> Adding to its sanctity, Mount Misen is the tallest peak at about 1,755 feet.<ref name="cali-2013a"/> Tourists can either hike or take a ropeway to the top.<ref name="cali-2013a"/>
Its treasures include the celebrated {{ill|en:List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)|en|en:List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)|qid=Q3245518|lt=Heike Nōkyō|1=List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)}}, or "Sutras dedicated by the [[Taira clan|House of Taira]]." These consist of thirty-two scrolls, on which the Lotus, Amida, and Heart sutras have been copied by Kiyomori, his sons, and other members of the family, each completing the transcription of one scroll, and it was "decorated with silver, gold, and mother-of-pearl by himself [Kiyomori] and other members of his clan."<ref name="cali-2013a"/>
Originally Itsukushima was a pure Shinto shrine "where no births or deaths were allowed to cause pollution". Because the island itself has been considered sacred, commoners were not allowed to set foot on it throughout much of its history to maintain its purity. Retaining the purity of the shrine is so important that since 1878, no deaths or births have been permitted near it.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.gojapango.com/travel/miyajima_itsukushima.htm|title=Itsukushima|year=2010|work=GoJapanGo|access-date=17 March 2011|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120609204136/http://www.gojapango.com/travel/miyajima_itsukushima.htm|archive-date=9 June 2012|url-status=dead}}</ref> To this day, pregnant women are supposed to retreat to the mainland as the day of delivery approaches, as are the terminally ill or the very elderly whose passing has become imminent. [[:en:Burials|Burials]] on the island are forbidden. To allow pilgrims to approach, the shrine was built like a {{ill|Pier|en|Pier|qid=Q863454|lt=Pier|1=Pier}} over the water, so that it appeared to float, separate from the land.<ref>{{Cite book|title=The Ritual Process: Structure and Anti-structure|url=https://archive.org/details/ritualprocess0000unse|url-access=registration|last=Turner|first=Victor W.|publisher=Aldine Pub.|year=1969|isbn=9780202010434|place=Chicago}}</ref> The red entrance gate, or ''[[torii]]'', was built over the water for much the same reason. Commoners had to steer their boats through the ''torii'' before approaching the shrine.[[Image:Itsukushima Shinto Shrine.jpg|center|700px|thumb|View from the ''[[torii]]'']]
== Architecture == [[File:Tori_gate_01.jpg|thumb|left|The ''torii'' gate, accessible from the island during low tide]]
Japan has gone to great lengths to preserve the twelfth-century-style architecture of the Shrine throughout history. The shrine was designed and built according to the ''{{ill|Shinden-zukuri|en|Shinden-zukuri|qid=Q2975225|lt=Shinden-zukuri|1=Shinden-zukuri}}'' style, equipped with pier-like structures over the {{ill|Matsushima|en|Matsushima|qid=Q1144855|lt=Matsushima|1=Matsushima}} bay in order to create the illusion of floating on the water, separate from island, which could be approached by the devout "like a palace on the sea."<ref name="sadler-2009a"/> This idea of intertwining architecture and nature is reflective of a popular trend during the 16th century as well as the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}} in which Japanese structures tended to "follow after their environment," often allowing trees, water, and other forms of natural beauty to enter into the decor of homes and buildings. That led to a far more intimate relationship between the two.<ref name="calza-2002a"/>
[[File:Itsukushima-jinja_honden.jpeg|thumb|Itsukushima {{lang|ja-latn|[[honden]]}}]] [[File:Itsukushima_Honden_Haiden.jpg|thumb|Itsukushima ''[[Haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'']]
The most recognizable and celebrated feature of the Itsukushima shrine, is its {{convert|50|ft|m|0|adj=on}}-tall vermilion otorii gate ("great gate"), built of decay-resistant [[:en:Cinnamomum camphora|camphor wood]].<ref name="cali-2013a"/> The placement of an additional leg in front of and behind each main pillar identifies the ''torii'' as reflecting the style of ''[[:en:Ryōbu Shintō|Ryōbu Shintō]]'' (dual Shinto), a medieval school of esoteric {{ill|Buddhism in Japan|en|Buddhism in Japan|qid=Q736311|lt=Buddhism in Japan|1=Buddhism in Japan}} associated with the {{ill|Shingon Buddhism|en|Shingon Buddhism|qid=Q550182|lt=Shingon Sect|1=Shingon Buddhism}}. The ''torii'' appears to be floating only at high {{ill|Tide|en|Tide|qid=Q23384|lt=Tide|1=Tide}}. When the tide is low, it is approachable by foot from the island. Gathering {{ill|Shellfish|en|Shellfish|qid=Q6501235|lt=Shellfish|1=Shellfish}} near the gate is popular at low tide. At night, powerful lights on the shore illuminate the structure. The current ''torii'' dates to 1875, although there has been one on that site since 1168.<ref name="cali-2013a" />
{{ill|Shinto architecture|en|Shinto architecture|qid=Q3080012|lt=Shinto architecture|1=Shinto architecture}} has many distinct parts, most of which include the shrine's {{lang|ja-latn|[[honden]]}} (main hall) and the unusually long ''[[Haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' (main oratory), and its equally long ''[[Heiden (Shinto)|heiden]]'' (offertory hall). The {{lang|ja-latn|honden}} "is an eight-by-four bay structure with a kirizuma roof surfaced in {{ill|Cypress|en|Cypress|qid=Q14169641|lt=Cypress|1=Cypress}} bark."<ref name="cali-2013a" /> Its walls are decorated in white {{ill|Stucco|en|Stucco|qid=Q33526|lt=Stucco|1=Stucco}}, and were constructed using a process requiring fifteen coats of white stucco, with vermilion woodwork.<ref name="cali-2013a" />
Extending from the sides of the ''haraiden'' of the main shrine is a ''{{ill|Noh|en|Noh|qid=Q226887|lt=Noh|1=Noh}}'' stage which dates from 1590.<ref name="nussbaum4072"/> ''Noh'' theater performances have long been used to pay homage to the gods through the ritual acting out of key events in Shinto myth.
On September 5, 2004, the shrine was severely damaged by [[2004 Pacific typhoon season#Typhoon Songda (Nina)|Typhoon Songda]]. The boardwalks and roof were partially destroyed, and the shrine was temporarily closed for repairs. Today anyone can visit the shrine at a cost of 300 yen.<ref name="cali-2013a" />
